title: Proverbs 28
subtitle: Wicked Flee; Confess and Find Mercy; Faithful vs. Avaricious; Give to the Poor

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — Wicked Flee; Confess and Find Mercy; Happy Is the Reverent

heb.paragraph: The wicked nās (flee) when no one pursues, but the righteous are bold as a lion (v. 1). Guilt produces phantom pursuers: the wicked live in constant anxiety because their conscience manufactures threats. The righteous, having nothing to hide, possess the boldness (biṭḥâ) of a young lion (kĕpîr). Moral integrity produces psychological freedom.

Whoever conceals transgressions will not prosper, but whoever yōdeh (confesses) and forsakes them will find mercy (v. 13). The verb yādâ in the Hiphil means to acknowledge openly, to throw out into the open. Confession in Proverbs is not ritual but relational — it combines acknowledgment with abandonment (ʿāzab). Half-measures do not receive mercy.

refs.note: They were overwhelmed with dread where there was nothing to dread — the same flight-without-pursuer psychology as v.1.
If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us — the NT fulfilment of v.13's mercy that follows genuine confession.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The wicked flee when no one pursues — Alter notes the psychological insight is one of Proverbs' sharpest: the guilty carry their own prosecution. The righteous are 'as bold as a lion' because they have nothing to flee from. The contrast is not between the powerful and the weak but between the clean-conscienced and the guilty.

Confess and find mercy — the two-part movement (confess AND renounce) is deliberate. Alter notes that confession without renunciation is incomplete acknowledgment; renunciation without confession may be self-sufficient pride. The combination is the covenant pattern of genuine repentance: honest acknowledgment of what was done paired with genuine turning from it.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The wicked flee though no one pursues — Waltke notes the self-generated flight of the guilty conscience is one of Proverbs' most psychologically acute observations. Guilt produces fearfulness without external cause. The person who violates covenant norms carries the awareness of that violation as a persistent source of anxiety that no external security can address.
Whoever conceals their sins does not prosper; whoever confesses and renounces finds mercy -- the covenant pattern of genuine repentance: honest acknowledgment plus genuine turning.

Calvin: The torment of a guilty conscience is its own prosecutor and its own punishment. The person who has offended God carries their condemnation within them. No external security, wealth, or power can silence the inner prosecution. This is simultaneously a warning (sin extracts its own punishment) and a gospel pointer (only forgiveness can silence the prosecution).

Calvin: Whoever confesses and renounces their sins will find mercy — one of Proverbs' most directly evangelical statements. Mercy is available but it requires two things: honest acknowledgment of failure (confession) and genuine turning from it (renunciation). Either without the other is insufficient.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the self-generated flight of the guilty discusses the psychological mechanism: guilt (awareness of covenant violation) produces fearfulness without any external threat. The note traces the contrast — wicked flee / righteous bold as a lion — as a character antithesis grounded in the presence or absence of moral integrity. The righteous have nothing to flee from; the wicked carry their prosecution within them.

The NET note on confession (modeh) and renunciation (azav, forsaking) together constituting genuine repentance traces the two-part structure through 1 Jn 1:9 (if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive) as the NT parallel. The structure is consistent: honest acknowledgment plus genuine turning equals the covenant pattern of restoration.

hist.context: The first half of ch.28 contains two of Proverbs' most psychologically precise observations: the wicked who flee though no one pursues (v.1) — the internal corruption that produces perpetual fear — and the mercy that follows genuine confession of sin (v.13). V.14's closing beatitude: blessed is the one who always trembles before God.

sections.header: Verses 15–28 — The Poor Over the Liar; Giving to the Poor Lacks Nothing; Who Trusts the LORD

heb.paragraph: Whoever trusts (bôṭēaḥ) in his own heart is a fool, but whoever walks in wisdom will be delivered (v. 26). The verb bāṭaḥ (to trust) is used positively throughout the Psalms — but here the object of trust is wrong. Self-trust is self-deception. The heart (lēb) that Proverbs has trained the reader to guard is the very thing that cannot be trusted as its own guide.

heb.gloss: one who gives to the poor
heb.paragraph: Whoever gives to the poor (lārāš) will not lack, but whoever hides his eyes will have many curses (v. 27). The rāš is the materially destitute person, and heʿlîm ʿênāyw (hiding the eyes) describes the deliberate act of looking away. Generosity in Proverbs is not charity but economics: the universe is designed so that giving produces abundance and hoarding produces scarcity.

refs.note: The generous paradox — those who give freely gain more. The same principle as v.27's giving-to-the-poor lacks nothing.
Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled — the NT application of v.26's trust-in-self as folly.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Faithful versus eager to get rich — Alter notes both appear to be pursuing wealth; the character of the pursuit makes all the difference. The faithful person builds through patient diligence; the avaricious person seeks shortcuts that compromise the very foundation of what they build. The contrast is in the character of the striving, not in the goal.

Those who give to the poor will lack nothing; those who close their eyes to them receive many curses — Alter notes the closing-eyes image is an active choice. The person who closes their eyes has decided not to see what they could not help but notice. This active avoidance is what attracts the many curses — not ignorance but deliberate refusal.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A faithful person will be richly blessed; one eager to get rich will not go unpunished — Waltke notes the antithesis is between covenant-faithful diligence and the avarice that cuts corners. Both appear to be pursuing wealth; the character of the pursuit determines the result. Faithful diligence builds sustainably; avarice sows the seeds of its own collapse.
Those who give to the poor will lack nothing; those who close their eyes to them receive many curses -- the closing-eyes image is active avoidance, not ignorance.

Calvin: A faithful man shall abound with blessings — faithfulness in small things produces the accumulation that hasty greed cannot achieve. The covenant-faithful person may prosper slowly; they prosper genuinely. The avaricious person appears to prosper quickly and loses quickly. The foundation determines the sustainability.

Calvin: Those who give to the poor will lack nothing; those who close their eyes to them receive many curses — there is no neutral position in Proverbs' poverty ethics. To close your eyes to the poor when you could help is not neutrality but a covenant violation that attracts the same curse as active exploitation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on aman (faithful, reliable, trustworthy) — covenant-faithful reliability in all things, including economic life — explains why faithfulness produces blessing. The person whose character is reliable attracts the trust and cooperation of the community, which produces the social capital that translates into material prosperity.

The NET note on the closing-eyes image explains that ra'ah (to see) in the negative form (not seeing/closing eyes) means active avoidance of awareness. The note discusses the covenantal implications: to see and not act is to take a moral position. Proverbs does not recognise neutrality in the face of the poor's need.

hist.context: The second half addresses the poor and their oppressors (vv.15,27), the person who hastens to get rich (v.22), and the section's central warning: the one who trusts in themselves is a fool, but the one who walks in wisdom is kept safe (v.26). The chapter closes with the giving-to-the-poor promise: those who give will lack nothing.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Whoever conceals their sins does not prosper, but the one who confesses and renounces them finds mercy."NoteAmong the most important pastoral verses in Proverbs. Concealment preserves the appearance of righteousness while the reality rots. Confession releases the pressure and opens the way to mercy.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Confess and Find Mercy
rows.text: Whoever conceals their sins does not prosper, but the one who confesses and renounces them finds mercy. Concealment blocks; confession + renunciation opens. The two-part movement is the covenant pattern of genuine repentance.

rows.label: Wicked Flee: The Psychology of Guilt
rows.text: The wicked flee though no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion. Guilt produces a fearfulness with no external cause. The guilty carry their own prosecution; the righteous person needs no external protection.

tx.title: Chapter 28 — LXX expansions on justice
tx.content: The LXX expands several proverbs in chapter 28, particularly those dealing with justice and governance. Where MT offers a terse observation about rulers, the LXX often adds a moral conclusion. This editorial pattern is consistent with the LXX translator's tendency throughout Proverbs.
tx.note: The LXX's justice-oriented expansions may reflect the concerns of the Hellenistic Jewish community in Alexandria, where questions of governance and law were particularly pressing.

positions.name: A general principle, not a transactional formula
positions.argument: The generosity-blessing and greed-punishment patterns describe how the moral order generally works, not guaranteed transactions.

positions.name: A theological principle with genuine economic dimensions
positions.argument: The covenant-blessing and covenant-curse patterns are real theological observations about how God orders material reality.

prayer_prompt: Whoever conceals their sins does not prosper, but the one who confesses finds mercy. Consider asking God about what you're concealing — what confession would open to mercy.

coaching.tip: 'The wicked flee though no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion' (v. 1). Guilt creates phantom pursuers; innocence creates courage. The wicked live in constant fear of exposure; the righteous have nothing to hide. Moral standing shapes psychological experience.

'Whoever conceals their sins does not prosper, but the one who confesses and renounces them finds mercy' (v. 13). Concealment compounds the original offense. Confession breaks the cycle. This isn't about groveling but about honesty that leads to freedom. Mercy awaits those who stop hiding.
